questions.question: Explain the role of digital signatures in the execution of a smart contract. (3 marks)
expectedPoints: Provides authentication, verifying the identity of the transaction sender.
Provides non-repudiation, ensuring the sender cannot deny authorizing the transaction.
Uses asymmetric cryptography (private/public keys) to sign transactions that interact with the contract.

questions.question: Discuss the 'immutability' of smart contracts. Explain why it is a critical feature for trust but also a significant challenge for developers. Provide an example of a historical incident related to this challenge. (10 marks)
expectedPoints: Definition: Immutability means the code cannot be changed once deployed to the blockchain.
Importance for Trust: Guarantees the contract will execute exactly as written, creating predictable and tamper-proof outcomes. Users don't need to trust a central party, only the code.
Challenge: If a bug or vulnerability exists in the code, it is permanent and can be exploited, potentially leading to massive financial losses.
Development Impact: Necessitates extreme rigor, including extensive testing, formal verification, and third-party audits before deployment.
Historical Example: The DAO hack on Ethereum (2016). A reentrancy vulnerability in the smart contract code was exploited to drain over $60 million worth of ETH.
Mitigation Strategies: Use of patterns like 'circuit breakers', upgradable proxy patterns (in some contexts), and simply deploying a new corrected contract.
